# Page 13^page=13
EPCIS and CBV Implementation Guideline Release 2.0 Ratified, Mar 2023 © 2022-2023 GS1 AISBL Page 13 of 93 1 Introduction 184 Consumers and businesses rely on global supply chains to produce a diverse array of high quality, 185 safe goods and services at affordable prices in a socially and environmentally responsible way. 186 Meeting the demands of today’s consumer requires a much finer degree of supply chain visibility 187 than has been typically exposed in the past. Increasingly, organisations, governments and 188 consumers want that ability to track and trace the products they purchase, the things they eat and 189 perhaps even electronic records about things they care about. 190 Visibility data can describe the origin of an object (virtual or physical), each location where it is 191 subject to a business process throughout the supply chain or other process, when those processes 192 took place and what was occurring to that object at each point. Visibility data is the WHAT, WHERE, 193 WHEN, WHY and HOW about an object. Capturing and sharing visibility data, either internally or 194 across trading partners provides a view into the history of the manufacture, shipping, receiving and 195 selling processes that allow for a more efficient, affordable and safe supply chain. 196 EPCIS is a GS1 standard that defines a common data model for visibility data and interfaces for 197 capturing and sharing visibility data within an enterprise and across an open supply chain. The goal 198 of EPCIS is to enable disparate applications to create and share visibility event data, both within and 199 across enterprises. Ultimately, this sharing is aimed at enabling users to gain a shared view of 200 physical or digital objects within a relevant business context. 201 1.1 Intended audience 202 This guide is intended to provide supply chain stakeholders, including manufacturers, distributors, 203 retailers, logistics providers, solution providers, business process architects, IT departments 204 (developers) and solution providers with an introduction to implementing a visibility system using 205 EPCIS and the Core Business Vocabulary (CBV) specifically, along with other GS1 standards. 206 1.2 Document scope 207 This guide was developed to provide both overview and guidance on getting started with visibility 208 systems using EPCIS. It is not intended to be a detailed, technical industry-specific "how to" guide. 209 Industries including Pharmaceutical, Electronics, Logistics and Food & Agriculture, have developed 210 industry specific implementation guides for EPCIS. This document intends to provide guidance at a 211 basic use or foundational level, allowing those guidelines to layer on their specific industry 212 requirements on top. 213 2 Overview of EPCIS 214 The goal of EPCIS is to enable disparate applications to create and share visibility event data, both 215 within and across enterprises. Ultimately, this sharing is aimed at enabling users to gain a shared 216 view of physical or digital objects within a relevant business context. 217 "Objects" in the context of EPCIS typically refers to physical objects that are identified either at a 218 class or instance level and which are handled in physical handling steps of an overall business 219 process involving one or more organisations. Examples of such physical objects include trade items 220 (products), logistic units, returnable assets, fixed assets, physical documents, etc. "Objects" may 221 also refer to digital objects, also identified at either a class or instance level, which participate in 222 comparable business process steps. Examples of such digital objects include digital trade items 223 (music downloads, electronic books, etc.), digital documents (electronic coupons, etc.), and so 224 forth. Throughout this document the word "object" is used to denote a physical or digital object, 225 identified at a class or instance level, that is the subject of a business process step. EPCIS data 226 consist of "visibility events," each of which is the record of the completion of a specific business 227 process step acting upon one or more objects. 228 The EPCIS standard was originally conceived as part of a broader effort to enhance collaboration 229 between trading partners by sharing of detailed information about physical or digital objects. The 230 name EPCIS reflects the origins of this effort in the development of the Electronic Product Code 231 (EPC). It should be noted, however, that EPCIS does not require the use of Electronic Product 232 Codes, and does not even require instance-level identification. EPCIS/CBV 2.0 permits the use of a 233

# Page 14^page=14
EPCIS and CBV Implementation Guideline Release 2.0 Ratified, Mar 2023 © 2022-2023 GS1 AISBL Page 14 of 93 constrained set of GS1 Digital Link URIs as an equivalent to existing EPC URNs or generic HTTP 234 URLs. 235 The EPCIS standard applies to all situations in which visibility event data is to be captured and 236 shared, and the presence of "EPC" within the name is of historical significance only. 237 2.1 What’s in the EPCIS and CBV standards? 238 The EPCIS standard defines: 239 ■ A data model for visibility event data, with XML (Extensible Markup Language), JSON (JavaScript 240 Object Notation) and JSON-LD (JavaScript Object Notation for Linked Data) syntax support. 241 ■ Open, standardised interfaces that allow for seamless integration of well-defined services in 242 inter-company environments as well as within companies. There are two interfaces defined in the 243 EPCIS standard: 244 □ A capture interface through which visibility event data conforming to the EPCIS data model 245 may be delivered from capturing applications to a receiver, typically a persistent repository of 246 EPCIS data; and 247 □ A query interface through which EPCIS event data may be requested by and delivered to a 248 business application or a trading partner. 249 Standard interfaces are defined in the EPCIS standard to enable visibility event data to be captured 250 and queried using a defined set of service operations and associated data standards, all combined 251 with appropriate security mechanisms that satisfy the needs of user companies. In many or most 252 cases, this will involve the use of one or more persistent databases of visibility event data, though a 253 direct linkage between capture and query interface could be used for direct application-to-254 application sharing without persistent databases. 255 EPCIS is intended to be used in conjunction with the CBV [CBV2.0]. The CBV provides definitions of 256 data values that may be used to populate the data structures defined in the EPCIS standard. The 257 use of the standardised vocabulary provided by the CBV standard is critical to interoperability and 258 critical to provide for querying of data by reducing the variation in how different businesses express 259 common intent. Therefore, capturing applications should use the CBV standard to the greatest 260 extent possible in constructing EPCIS data. 261 2.2 Example of EPCIS Visibility Data 262 EPCIS data is intended to provide information systems with visibility as to where objects are (and 263 have been) within the business processes in which those things are handled. The following figure 264 illustrates a simple business process, showing where EPCIS data may be generated. 265

# Page 15^page=15
EPCIS and CBV Implementation Guideline Release 2.0 Ratified, Mar 2023 © 2022-2023 GS1 AISBL Page 15 of 93 Figure 2-1 Simple Business Process Showing Generation of EPCIS Data 266 267 This figure illustrates a simple business process in which a trade item is manufactured and shipped 268 to a distribution centre, where it is subsequently received and later shipped to a retail store, where 269 it is received and later moved into the sales area. The entire business process may be viewed as a 270 sequence of individual business steps: product packaging, packing into a shipping container, 271 shipping, receiving, and so on. EPCIS data can provide a detailed record of any or all of these steps. 272 A unit of EPCIS data that describes the completion of one business step is called an EPCIS event, 273 and a collection of EPCIS events provides a detailed picture of a business process over time and 274 place. 275 For example, a single EPCIS event records the receipt of one shipment at the distribution centre. 276 The information content of this event is organised into five dimensions: 277 ■ What: Information about what trade items and/or shipping containers were received 278 ■ When: The date and time when receiving occurred, and the local time zone in effect 279 ■ Where: The location where the shipment was received, and where the items are expected to be 280 following the event 281 ■ Why: Information about the business context, including: 282 □ indication that the business step is a receiving operation (as opposed to shipping or some 283 other business step); 284 □ information on the asset's status (e.g., that the shipment is in transit)t; 285 □ the identity of the shipping and receiving locations, as well as the identity of the source and 286 destination parties that are involved in possession or ownership; 287 □ Links to relevant business transaction documents, such as a purchase order, an invoice, a 288 despatch advice (a.k.a. advance ship notice), etc. 289 ■ How: Sensor-based conditional information, captured – for example, during refrigerated 290 transport, or in the retailer's cold storage room – either in predefined time intervals or when a 291 specific temperature threshold is exceeded. 292 Each of the business steps in the process illustrated in the figure above could be the source of an 293 EPCIS event. The details of the content of each of those events are different depending on the 294 business step, but all have the same four- or five-dimensional structure. 295 Manufacturer Retailer DC Retail Store Ship Door Rcv Door Ship Door Rcv Door Pack Line Front Back V1 V2 V3 V4 V5 V7 V8 EPCIS Visibility Data Front Cold Storage V6

# Page 16^page=16
EPCIS and CBV Implementation Guideline Release 2.0 Ratified, Mar 2023 © 2022-2023 GS1 AISBL Page 16 of 93 2.3 EPCIS in business applications 296 The power of EPCIS lies in bringing together individual events that are recorded over time and 297 across a complete business process and/or supply chain. Examples of such paradigms include: 298 ■ Finding the most recent EPCIS event for a given object, to learn where it currently is and what 299 state it is in ("tracking"); 300 ■ Assembling a history of events for a given object, to understand its path through an overall 301 business process or supply chain ("tracing"); 302 ■ Analysing a collection of events gathered over time at a particular location or within a particular 303 business process; ("analysis") 304 ■ Comparing the actual status of objects based on a current EPCIS event to what was expected to 305 have happened based on a prior business transaction or a prior EPCIS event; ("checking") 306 ■ Triggering other business processes in real time based on what a freshly captured EPCIS event 307 reveals about the completion of a business step ("automation"). 308 Below are examples of business applications that can benefit from EPCIS data, along with the 309 paradigm involved. Section 4 describes the design process in more detail, showing how this eventually results in EPCIS 659 data conforming to the standard. 660 4 Designing a Visibility system using EPCIS 661 Building visibility systems requires both technical understanding of the EPCIS standard and a 662 structured methodology. The following methodology is used to analyse a visibility process from a 663 business perspective regardless of the technology used to capture events. Once a process is fully 664 mapped, visibility events are identified and described. The technical details at the device level are 665 omitted in this guide since we are primarily concerned with the business application of EPCIS data. 666 The visibility modelling methodology has these steps: 667 1. Collect visibility goals and requirements 668 2. Document the business process flows 669 3. Break each process flow into a series of discrete business steps 670 4. Decide which business steps require visibility events 671 5. Model the completion of each step as a visibility event - Understand what information is needed 672 from a business application’s perspective 673 6. Decide what data fields are to be included in the visibility event 674 a. Start with standard EPCIS data fields 675 b. Define extension fields if necessary 676 7. Determine the vocabularies that populate each data field according to section 7 and 8 of the CBV 677 standard 678 8. It is tempting to approach this from the 835 perspective of what information is available to your capturing application, such as what data comes 836 out of an RFID reader or bar code scanner. However, EPCIS data is much more useful if you 837 approach it from the opposite direction, namely from the perspective of a business application 838 consuming the data. The question to ask yourself is: "what information does a business application 839 need to understand what happened during this event?" The business application doesn’t need to 840 know how the data was captured; it needs to know what happened from a business perspective. 841 A good way to proceed is to consider each of the four data dimensions in turn. 842 4.6.1 Designing the What Dimension 843 The What dimension identifies the physical or digital objects involved in the event. The structure of 844 the information in the What dimension depends on the event type: 845 ■ For an ObjectEvent, the What dimension contains a list of objects. All objects participate in the 846 event in the same way. 847 ■ For an AggregationEvent, the What dimension names a specific object as the "parent" and 848 contains a list of other objects as the "children." (There are two exceptions. If the action is 849 OBSERVE the parent may be omitted, indicating that the children were observed in a state of 850 aggregation but the identity of the parent is unknown. If the action is DELETE the children may 851 be omitted, indicating that all children are disaggregated from the parent.) 852 ■ For a TransformationEvent, the What dimension includes one list of objects that are the 853 inputs to the transformation, and a second list of (different) objects that are the outputs of the 854 transformation. (If a TransformationEvent is connected to other TransformationEvents through 855 the TransformationID, it may omit either the inputs or the outputs; see section 5.5.2.) 856 Besides considering which objects involved the business process step are relevant to the event, you 857 also have to determine how those objects will be named in the event. In EPCIS there are two 858 different ways to refer to an object: 859 ■ Instance-level Identification: If an object has an identifier that is unique to that particular 860 object, it is called instance-level identification. Examples of instance-level identification include a 861 Global Trade Item Number (GTIN) with a serial number (together called a Serialised GTIN, or 862 SGTIN), a Serial Shipping Container Code (SSCC), a Global Returnable Asset Identifier (GRAI) 863 that includes a serial number, and so on. 864 ■ Class-level Identification: If an object has an identifier that is identical to the identifier 865 carried by other, similar objects, it is called class-level identification. Examples of class-level 866 identification include a GTIN plus a batch or lot number (shared by all trade items belonging to 867 the same batch or lot), a GTIN by itself, a GRAI without a serial number, and so on. 868 Instance-level identification is the most powerful in terms of how EPCIS data can be used by 869 applications, because instance-level identification makes it possible to recognise that an object 870 referenced in one event is the very same object as an object referenced in a prior or subsequent 871 event. On the other hand, assigning instance-level identification to objects is usually a more 872 complex business process than assigning class-level identification. 873 When class-level identification is used there may be more than one object involved in the event 874 from the same class, so a class-level identifier is usually accompanied by information that specifies 875 the quantity. It is required in every 892 event, and always contains two pieces of information: 893 ■ EventTime: The date and time at which the event occurred. This is always expressed in a 894 format that includes a time zone specifier, so that it unambiguously identifies a moment in time. 895 ■ EventTimeZoneOffset: The time zone offset (relative to UTC) that was in effect at the place 896 where the event took place. This allows the EventTime to be displayed to users in the local time 897 where the event happened, if desired. 898 The correct value to use for these two data elements is usually quite obvious and so there is little 899 design work to be done. 900 For a business step that takes place over a long interval of time, there may be some question as to 901 whether EventTime should be the moment when the step begins or ends, or some moment in 902 between. Usually, the ending time of the business step is the most appropriate. But as with all 903 EPCIS data design questions, it should be considered from the perspective of a business application 904 consuming the data. If it is important to business applications to know both the starting time and 905 the ending time of a business step, you should consider whether it would be more appropriate to 906 model the process using two EPCIS events, one for the start of the process and one for the end. 907 Conversely, sometimes there are several different events from a business perspective which are 908 carried out simultaneously or in a way that would make it difficult to assign a different EventTime 909 for each. For example, an automated manufacturing machine might assign SGTINs to twelve 910 products ("commissioning" business step), assign another SGTIN to a case ("commissioning" again), 911 and pack the items into the case ("packing" business step), all at once. It may not be physically all 912 at once, but the EPCIS Capturing Application built into the machine may not have any way to 913 distinguish the times. In such cases it may be appropriate to assign the identical event time to all 914 EPCIS events generated, but if there is a logical sequencing of the events it is usually much better 915 for consuming applications if the event times are slightly altered so that the chronological order is 916 logical. In the items-into-case example, the EPCIS event for "packing" (the aggregation event) 917 should have an event time that is later than the commissioning events, even if it is artificially set to 918 a time only one millisecond later. This allows consuming applications to order the events by their 919 EventTime to arrive at a logical sequence. 920

For example, the ReadPoint 929 could be just as relevant from a business perspective, or more so, than the BusinessLocation, 930 depending on the situation. Instead of trying to read meanings into the names "read point" and 931 "business location", just remember the definitions: ReadPoint is the location of the objects at the 932 time of the event, BusinessLocation is the location afterwards. 933 The difference between ReadPoint and BusinessLocation can be visualised by imagining a facility 934 having several rooms connected by doorways, like this: 935 936 Imagine that an EPCIS event is captured whenever an object moves through one of the doorways; 937 e.g., by an RFID reader stationed at each door. In this case, the ReadPoint (the location at the time of the event) is 939 Door A and the BusinessLocation (the location afterward) is Room 2. Note that the object might 940 move around within Room 2 without generating any new EPCIS events, so at the time it moved into 941 Room 2 all we know about where it is afterwards is that it is somewhere within Room 2. If instead 942 the object had moved from Room 1 to Room 2 via Door B the BusinessLocation would still be 943 Room 2 but the ReadPoint would be Door B. On the other hand, if the object later moves in the 944 opposite direction through Door A the ReadPoint would again be Door A but the BusinessLocation 945 would be Room 1. 946 The reason it is useful to have BusinessLocation is that it helps to answer the question "where is the 947 object right now?" At the same time, ReadPoint is 950 useful because it tells you something about the past: "where was the object when X happened to 951 it?" (where X is described by the Why dimension of the appropriate event). 952 A key question in designing the Where dimension is to decide at what granularity you will describe 953 location. The BusinessStep value is an identifier that says what step 998 of the business process was taking place at the time of the event. Without the business step an 999 application only knows that an object existed at a particular place ant time; with the business step 1000 an application knows how that object relates to the overall business. The CBV is one 1007 such standard. It is a global standard that defines several dozen business step values that apply to a 1008 variety of business steps commonly occurring in supply chain business processes across many 1009 industry sectors. Because it is a global, cross-sector standard, using CBV business step values 1010

The net effect is that the collection of 1881 all events pertaining to a specific object (a "trace") should correctly indicate the history and current 1882 state of that object, by interpreting the events according to the semantics specified in the EPCIS and 1883 CBV standards, and any other relevant vocabulary standards. 1884 Sometimes, it is discovered that an event recorded earlier does not accurately reflect what 1885 happened in the real world. However, neither the EPCIS Capture Interface nor the EPCIS Query 1886 Interface provides a means by which an application can delete or modify an EPCIS Event. The only 1887 way to "retract" or "correct" an EPCIS Event is to generate a subsequent event whose business 1888 meaning is to rescind or amend the effect of a prior event. The net effect is that the complete trace 1889 (including the new events and all prior events including the incorrect event) accurately reflects the 1890 history and current state, as stated in the above principle. 1891 The preferred way to arrive at the additional events is to recognise that the discovery of an 1892 erroneous event and its remediation is itself a business process which can be modelled by creating 1893 suitable EPCIS events. In most situations, this is done using the same methods discussed in 1894 section 4. 1895 Example 1: Company X records an EPCIS event asserting that serial numbers 101, 102, and 103 of 1896 some product were shipped to Company Y. Company Y receives the shipment and finds serial 1897 number 104 in addition to serial numbers 101, 102, 103. In discussion with Company X, it is agreed 1898 that serial 104 was indeed shipped and that the shipping event was in error. Remediation: Company 1899 X records a new EPCIS event asserting that serial number 104 was shipped, with similar contextual 1900 information as the original event. 1901 Example 2: Company X records an EPCIS event asserting that serial numbers 101, 102, and 103 of 1902 some product were shipped to Company Y. Company Y receives the shipment and finds only serial 1903 numbers 101, 102. In discussion with Company X, it is agreed that serial 103 was not shipped but 1904 remains in Company X's inventory. They agree to reverse the billing for the third product. 1905 Remediation: Company X records a new EPCIS event asserting that the shipment of serial 103 is 1906 voided. 1907 In the first example, the additional event uses the same business vocabulary as the first. In the 1908 second example, vocabulary specifically associated with the process of voiding a shipment is used, 1909 but it is still "ordinary" EPCIS semantics in the sense that it models the completion of a well-defined 1910 business process step. This reflects the reality that the act remediation is itself a business process, 1911 and so may be modelled as an EPCIS event. 1912 In some situations, it either is not possible (or is highly undesirable) to remediate the history of an 1913 object by creating a new EPCIS event with ordinary semantics. 1914 Example 3: Company X records an EPCIS event to assert that serial number 101 of product X was 1915 destroyed. This event is an Object Event with action = DELETE. Later it is discovered that serial 101 1916

Specifically, 2003 given Event E1, an error declaration event E2 whose effect is to declare the assertions of E1 to be in 2004 error is an event structure whose content is identical to E1, but with the ErrorDeclaration element 2005 included. For example, the error declaration for the "destroying" event in Example 3 is also an 2006 Object Event with action = DELETE, but with the ErrorDeclaration element included. In general, 2007 to declare event E to be in error, a new event is recorded that is identical to event E except that the 2008 ErrorDeclaration element is also included (and the record time will be different). 2009 There are three reasons why error declaration events in EPCIS are expressed this way. One, an 2010 event ID is not required to indicate the erroneous event, which in turn implies it is not necessary to 2011 include an event ID on every event to provide for possible error declaration in the future. Event IDs 2012 are available to link an error declaration event to a corrective event, but it is never necessary to use 2013 event IDs. Two, any EPCIS query that matches an event will also match an error declaration for that 2014 event, if it exists. This means that EPCIS Accessing Applications require no special logic to become 2015 aware of error declarations, if they exist. Three, if an EPCIS Accessing Application receives an error 2016 declaration event and for some reason does not have a copy of the original (erroneous) event, it is 2017 not necessary to retrieve the original event as every bit of information in that event is also present 2018 in the error declaration event. 2019

This is useful to have precise 2023 visibility on which items were built into which products, assemblies, or assets. 2024 For instance, applying Association Events is applicable in the following situations: 2025 ■ installation of a sensor device into a reusable plastic tray 2026 ■ construction of a rail wagon (which is made up by axles, bogies, roof components, brake 2027 systems, buffers, etc.) 2028 ■ removing a (defect) component from an assembly 2029 Prior to EPCIS 2.0, companies had to use AggregationEvents for such use cases. However, the 2030 EPCIS standard allows to capture an AggregationEvent with an 2031 empty childEPCs and/or childQuantityList element when the action value is DELETE. Note though 2032 that plastic trays or rail waggons can also be used for more temporary aggregations such as in the 2033 course of loading or packing events. If an EPCIS-based visibility system used AggregationEvents 2034 also for the construction of transport units and captured an unloading or unpacking event with an 2035 empty childEPCs and/or childQuantityList element, it would mean that not just the packed or loaded 2036 objects were disaggregated from these transport units, but all the items the transport units 2037 themselves are made of, too. 2038 5.10.1 Example 1: Installing components/assemblies into larger items 2039 For illustration purposes, presume a pool operator of reuseable plastic trays wants to properly 2040 document the installation of sensor devices (with e.g. a built-in GPS module and temperature 2041 sensor) into its trays. The reason could consist in the need to effectively identify all assets that are 2042 equipped with specific sensor devices/models in case the latter were not exactly calibrated. In 2043 addition, information on built-in sensor devices may also be enquired by customs authorities. In 2044 such a scenario, the installing EPCIS event could be modelled as follows: 2045 EPCIS dimension Data Element V1 Description Installing a sensor device in a reusable plastic tray Event Type Association Event Action ADD When eventTime 12 October, 08:45 am What parentID GRAI of tray childEPCs GIAI of sensor device Where readPoint GLN of maintenance area Why bizStep installing 2046 5.10.2 Example 2: Installing components/assemblies into physical locations 2047 The AssociationEvent is the only EPCIS event type where it is permissible to populate 2048 the parentID field with a physical location identifier. This feature is especially relevant for 2049 companies that need to document which particular item became an integral part of a physical 2050 location. 2051

An end-of-life event is an Object Event with Action DELETE. 2167 Since bizLocation is the location where the object is presumed to be following the event, the 2168 bizLocation for an object which has undergone an end-of-life event is undefined and shall be omitted 2169 from the event. 2170 Several business steps from the CBV signify end-of-life events for the instance-level identification of 2171 an object: decommissioning, destroying, and dispensing. 2172 When an object's instance-level identifier is decommissioned: 2173 • the instance-level identifier ceases to exist even though the object may still physically exist. 2174 • the identifier's link to the physical object ceases to exist. 2175 A decommissioned identifier should not appear in EPCIS events thereafter, nor can a 2176 decommissioned identifier be reactivated. 2177 An object whose identifier has been decommissioned can be newly commissioned with a new 2178 instance-level identifier (e.g., using an EPCIS Transformation Event). 2179 5.13 Inferred completeness 2180 EPCIS events are typically used to record structured event data about activities in the real world, 2181 including observations and completion of business steps, often triggered by the scan of one or more 2182 barcodes or the detection of one or more RFID tags. 2183 There are occasions when an EPCIS event expresses the identifiers of objects whose barcodes or 2184 RFID tags have not actually been observed at that point in time but are considered to be 2185 present, based on prior event data or other information, combined with physical inspection that 2186 tamper-evident seals have not been broken. Such data may be used by many different business 2212 applications. This section discusses some of the practical aspects of sharing EPCIS data, both 2213 sharing with applications within one organisation’s four walls, and sharing between trading partners 2214 to achieve overall supply chain or ecosystem process visibility. 2215 6.1 EPCIS Queries 2216 EPCIS Accessing Applications obtain EPCIS events from an EPCIS Repository by means of an EPCIS 2217 Query. An EPCIS Query is a set of event-matching criteria specified by the application; the EPCIS 2218 Repository responds to a query by retrieving all EPCIS events that match the specified criteria. 2219 The EPCIS Standard, section 8.2.7.1, defines over 40 different criteria that can be used to construct 2220 a query for event data. These criteria can be used alone or in combination. Each criterion has a 2221 "parameter name" specified in the EPCIS standard, and most take a "parameter value" that further 2222 defines how the criterion is to be applied. The following table lists some of the commonly used 2223 criteria; see the EPCIS Standard, Section 8.2.7.1, for the complete list: 2224 Table 6-1 Selected EPCIS Query Criteria 2225 Query Criterion Parameter Name Query Criterion Parameter Value Description eventType One or more event types: ObjectEvent, AggregationEvent, TransactionEvent, TransformationEvent or AssociationEvent Matches events whose event type is one of the event types named in the parameter value. EQ_action One or more action values: ADD, OBSERVE, or DELETE Matches events that include an action and where the action is one of the actions named in the parameter value GE_eventTime A date/time value (including a time zone specifier) Matches events whose event time is on or after the date/time specified in the parameter value. LT_eventTime A date/time value (including a time zone specifier) Matches events whose event time is prior to the date/time specified in the parameter value. MATCH_anyEPC One or more instance-level identifiers, or patterns matching instance-level identifiers Matches events whose what dimension contains at least one instance-level identifier that matches one of the identifiers or patterns specified in the parameter value. MATCH_anyEPC looks for matching instance-level identifiers anywhere in the what dimension. Other query criteria are defined in the EPCIS standard that match specific parts of the what dimension; e.g. matching just the parent of an aggregation event but not children.

There are different ways in which the transfer can be 2236 triggered. 2237 ■ Pull: This method of transfer involves a request/response pattern. The application or trading 2238 partner issues a request to an EPCIS repository containing EPCIS query criteria, and the EPCIS 2239 repository responds with the EPCIS events that match the criteria. 2240 ■ Push: This method of transfer involves a one-way message: the EPCIS repository simply 2241 delivers one or more EPCIS events to an application or trading partner that needs them. There 2242 are two variations to this theme: 2243 □ Pre-arrangement: The sending and receiving party have agreed in advance, by some 2244 means outside of the scope of the EPCIS standard, what data the receiving party needs and 2245 under what conditions. The sending party delivers events when it sees fit based on that prior 2246 arrangement. 2247 □ Subscription: The receiving party issues a standing query to the sending party to express 2248 an ongoing information need. A standing query includes EPCIS query criteria (just as in the 2249 "pull" method) along with description of the conditions that will trigger the delivery of 2250 events. These conditions could be a regular schedule (e.g., daily at 3am) or some other 2251 triggering event. Each time the triggering condition occurs, the sending party evaluates the 2252 query criteria and delivers any new events that match the criteria (new compared to the last 2253 time the subscription was triggered). 2254 In both "push" variations, EPCIS events are delivered in a one-way communication from sender to 2255 receiver; the difference is that in the pre-arrangement variation the sender is in full control of what 2256 data is sent whereas in the subscription variation the receiver gets to express its needs via the 2257 standing query. Generally speaking, "push" methods are often used 2261 when there is a recurring predictable need for transfer of EPCIS data, and "pull" methods are used 2262 when transfer is needed unpredictably or only on an exception basis. Each party may be 2278 trading with many others, and each such trading relationship may require the exchange of EPCIS 2279 data. Moreover, it may be necessary for one party to share EPCIS data with another party with 2280 whom there is not a direct trading relationship; for example, if A sells to B and B sells to C, there 2281 may be a need for A and C to share EPCIS data to get a complete picture of the supply chain, even 2282 though A and C do not have a direct trading relationship. 2283 A core principle for managing this complexity is to separate content from choreography. What this 2284 means is that the content of EPCIS data – the specific business steps that require visibility, the 2285 EPCIS events that will used to record the completion of those steps, and the detailed contents of 2286 those events – should be designed according to the methods described earlier in this guideline 2287 (sections 3, 4, and 5). These methods focus on accurately modelling the what, when, where, why 2288 and, where applicable, how information for each business step. Separately from that, trading 2289 partners can decide when and how the data will move from one trading partner to another – this is 2290 called the choreography. Choreography decisions include: where will data reside, what will trigger 2291 the communication of data from one party to another, will push or pull modes be used, what 2292 networking technology will be used, and so forth. By separating content from choreography, the 2293 choreography can adapt to changes in the size of the trading ecosystem and evolution of 2294 technology, while the design of the EPCIS content stays the same. 2295 There are many possible approaches to choreography. Many of these approaches fall into one of the 2296 following three broad categories: 2297 ■ Centralised Choreography: In these models, EPCIS events from multiple parties in the supply 2298 chain are sent to a shared repository. To get an overall view of the supply chain, it is only 2299 necessary to query the shared repository. 2300 ■ Distributed Query Choreography: In these models, each party that captures EPCIS data 2301 keeps that data in its own repository. When another party needs an overall view of the supply 2302 chain, it must locate and query all of the other parties who may have relevant data within their 2303 respective repositories. 2304 ■ Distributed Push Choreography: In these models, each party that captures EPCIS data keeps 2305 that data in its own repository. But rather than waiting for another party to query for that data, 2306 the capturing party sends (pushes) its data to other parties in the supply chain who are likely to 2307 need that data. Often the push of data follows the same path as the physical or digital objects; 2308 e.g. if a Party A ships goods to Party B, it also sends its EPCIS data to Party B. 2309 The following sections illustrate examples of these three approaches in more detail. Throughout 2310 these sections, a scenario is illustrated in which Party A ships goods to Party B who ships goods to 2311 Party C, and upon receipt Party C would like to examine the upstream EPCIS events from A and B. 2312 Differences between choreography approaches are illustrated through the following four questions: 2313 ■ Questions for the producers of EPCIS event data: 2314

In order to interpret the business meaning of an 2408 EPCIS event, a business application typically needs additional descriptive information associated 2409 with each identifier. For example, descriptive information for a GTIN might include the name of the 2410 product, the brand name, the physical dimensions, and so on. Descriptive information for a GLN 2411 might include the street address of the location and its geocoordinates. Such descriptive information 2412 is called "master data." 2413 Compared to EPCIS event data, master data is static. Unlike event data, more master data is not 2414 created merely because more business is transacted. Master data is not completely static, however: 2415 additional master data may be created due to growth, for example when new products are 2416 introduced or new physical locations are built. But in general, a given identifier having a single set of 2417 associated master data may be mentioned in many different EPCIS events. For this reason, it is 2418 desirable to communicate master data in advance, just one time for each distinct identifier, rather 2419 than include master data in every EPCIS event. 2420 There are several ways that master data can be communicated from the creator of an identifier to 2421 the other parties who may need the master data. These include: 2422 ■ Using a system designed for the efficient communication of master data. Such systems include: 2423 □ The GS1 Global Data Synchronisation Network (GDSN), for both trade item (GTIN) master 2424 data and GLN master data 2425 □ The GS1 GLN Registry federation, for more detailed information about GLNs 2426 ■ Using the Instance/Lot Master Data (ILMD) feature of EPCIS events to carry master data directly 2427 within an event. This applies to master data for specific lots of a GTIN or to specific instances 2428 (SGTINs or other instance-level identifiers). 2429 ■ Using the VocabularyList element of the standard EPCIS Header to carry master data in an 2430 EPCIS XML document. 2431 ■ Other means not standardised by GS1. 2432 Of these methods, GDSN and the GLN Registry are fully governed by standards and so offer the 2433 greatest degree of interoperability. The ILMD feature, and – for the EPCIS XML binding – the 2434 VocabularyList element of the EPCIS Header provide a standardised interface for master data, 2435 and may be used with the master data attributes defined in the CBV. 2436

Therefore, merely because one party queries 2455 another party for EPCIS events matching some criteria does not mean that the queried party is 2456 obligated to respond with all matching events. Instead, the queried party may choose to restrict 2457 what data the querying party receives based on business rules. This is termed "redaction." 2458 In general, an EPCIS service that is sending data to another party, whether in response to a query 2459 or due to some other trigger, may consider the identity of the receiving party and apply business 2460 rules to redact the data. The following possibilities for redaction are paraphrased from the EPCIS 1.1 2461 standard, section 8.2.2 2462 ■ The service may refuse to honour the request altogether, by responding with a Security 2463 Exception 2464 ■ The service may respond with less data than requested. For example, if a querying party 2465 presents a query requesting all Object Event instances within a specified time interval, the 2466 service knows of 100 matching events, the service may choose to respond with fewer than 100 2467 events (e.g., returning only those events whose EPCs are SGTINs with a company prefix known 2468 to be assigned to the querying party). 2469 ■ The service may respond with coarser grained information. In particular, when the response to a 2470 query includes a location identifier the service may substitute an aggregate location in place of a 2471 primitive location (for example, a site-level GLN instead of the SGLN of a particular loading 2472 dock). 2473 ■ The service may hide information. For example, if a querying party presents a query requesting 2474 Object Event instances, the service may choose to delete the bizTransactionList fields in its 2475 response. The information returned, however, shall always be well-formed EPCIS events 2476 consistent with this specification and industry guidelines. For example, given an 2477 AggregationEvent with action equal to ADD, an attempt to hide the parentID field would result 2478 in a non-well-formed event, because parentID is required when the action is ADD; in this 2479 instance, therefore, either the parentID would have to be included or the entire event would 2480 have to be withheld. 2481 ■ The service may limit the scope of the query to data that was originally captured by a particular 2482 client identity. This allows a single EPCIS service to be partitioned for use by groups of unrelated 2483 users whose data should be kept separate (a so-called "multi-tenant" implementation). 2484 An EPCIS implementation is free to determine which if any of these actions to take in processing any 2485 query, using any means it chooses. The specification of authorisation rules is outside the scope of 2486 the EPCIS standard: the EPCIS standard does not take a position as to how authorisation decisions 2487 are taken. Particular implementations of EPCIS may have arbitrarily complex business rules for 2488 authorisation. 2489

For this purpose, organisations should apply validation mechanisms. These include technical, 2493 content, and integrity validation: 2494 ■ Technical validation implies that the EPCIS events conform to the current EPCIS standard 2495 from a technical perspective. In other words, events are transmitted in XML format according to 2496 the XML schema (XSD) specified in the EPCIS 1.1 standard. For specific use cases involving user 2497 or vendor extensions, best practise is to build a XSD covering the extra namespaces and XML 2498 elements required for these use cases and consider it for technical validation as well. 2499 ■ Content validation requires verification that discrete events make sense from a business 2500 perspective. For example, if the process flow in a specific use cases specifies that a pallet 2501 packing event should include an SSCC and a quantity of cases described with an LGTIN, then 2502 content validation would confirm that the packing event has that structure and not some other 2503 structure (which may be syntactically valid, but not appropriate for the specific use case). 2504 Additionally, a capture application of an EPCIS might perform semantic checks like date 2505 validation, for example to confirm the value of eventTime is not in the future. 2506 ■ Integrity validation requires that the visibility system operates end-to-end in the way 2507 described by the process map and achieves the desired business results. For example, a 2508 requirement could be that it is possible to trace back an item from the goods issue to the 2509 receiving process within a location. 2510 Both technical and content validation can usually be accomplished at the very moment EPCIS events 2511 are submitted via the EPCIS capture interface. Depending on how important data quality is, an 2512 EPCIS repository or capturing application may only accept incoming EPCIS events if they fulfil a 2513 predefined set of technical and content validation criteria (and reject them otherwise). Alternatively, 2514 incoming events could be accepted as long as they have passed the technical validation, with 2515 warnings generated if the content validation has failed. 2516 Integrity validation however can only be accomplished retrospectively, that is, after all events 2517 comprising an end-to-end-process have been captured. A business application which consumes 2518 visibility event data may apply appropriate rules to deal with invalid event sequences. Amongst 2519 other things, it may trigger an alert if a mandatory event to a specific business process does not 2520 exists, or it may disregard events which are obvious duplicates or which have no significance. 2521 7.2 Certification program 2522 The EPCglobal Software Certification Program is a standards-based compliance testing program, 2523 developed by the EPCglobal community to provide a neutral and authoritative source for testing 2524 EPC/RFID software products and providing information regarding certified products and the vendors 2525 who develop them. 2526 7.3 Requirements of program certification 2527 EPC Information Services (EPCIS) 1.0 Specification Conformance Requirements are published at 2528 http://www.gs1.org/gsmp/kc/epcglobal/epcis. 2529 7.4 Non-normative tools 2530 GS1 maintains a set of non-normative tools at https://ref.gs1.org/tools/ , with EPCIS-specific tools 2531 linked from https://ref.gs1.org/tools/epcis/ . 2532 8 References 2533 [CBV] GS1, "Core Business Vocabulary (CBV) Standard, Release 2.0," GS1 Standard, June 2022, 2534 https://ref.gs1.org/standards/cbv/. 2535
